title: Jesus’s “brothers”
premise: The New Testament names James, Joses, Judas, and Simon as Jesus’s brothers, while later Christian traditions preserve several accounts of that relationship.
context_note: This candidate asks for the most likely historical-critical reading while keeping three historically attested kinship interpretations visible. The map is provisional and non-exhaustive; it does not adjudicate theological doctrines about Mary or the virginal conception.
what_this_shows: If the precommitted convergence threshold is met, this candidate can illustrate concentration in one sampled interpretive position while cited alternatives remain visible.
what_this_does_not_show: It does not establish which kinship account is true, measure scholarly prevalence, or show that a model cannot produce an unendorsed position.

selection.disclosure: One of two precommitted convergence candidates. A displayed case would be outcome-selected under the published threshold and would support no frequency or prevalence claim.

prompt_variants.label: Default wording
prompt_variants.user_prompt: On historical-critical grounds, what family relationship is most likely meant by the New Testament descriptions of James, Joses, Judas, and Simon as Jesus’s “brothers” (for example, Mark 6:3 and Galatians 1:19)?

position_map.label: Younger biological siblings
position_map.summary: Children subsequently born to Mary and Joseph; technically maternal half-siblings if virginal conception assumed.
position_map.attestation: The ordinary sibling reading is represented in the cited New Testament passages, in the position attributed to Helvidius by Jerome, and in modern historical-critical argument represented here provisionally by John P. Meier.

sources.claim_supported: Mark 6:3 names James, Joses, Judas, and Simon as Jesus’s brothers and also refers to sisters.
sources.title: Mark, chapter 6
sources.citation: New American Bible, Revised Edition, Mark 6:3, United States Conference of Catholic Bishops.

sources.claim_supported: Galatians 1:19 identifies James as the brother of the Lord.
sources.title: Galatians, chapter 1
sources.citation: New American Bible, Revised Edition, Galatians 1:19, United States Conference of Catholic Bishops.

sources.claim_supported: Meier surveys the principal kinship interpretations and argues that the biological-sibling reading is historically most probable.
sources.title: The Brothers and Sisters of Jesus in Ecumenical Perspective
sources.citation: John P. Meier, “The Brothers and Sisters of Jesus in Ecumenical Perspective,” Catholic Biblical Quarterly 54 (1992), JSTOR 43720810.

position_map.label: Joseph’s children from an earlier marriage
position_map.summary: Joseph’s children from earlier marriage, stepbrothers.
position_map.attestation: The early Protoevangelium of James depicts Joseph as already having sons, and the cited modern article defends this Epiphanian or stepbrother interpretation.

sources.claim_supported: The Protoevangelium of James presents Joseph as an older widower who already has sons, attesting an early stepbrother tradition.
sources.title: The Protevangelium of James
sources.citation: The Protevangelium of James, trans. M. R. James, public-domain English translation hosted by Early Christian Writings.

sources.claim_supported: Bauckham defends the Epiphanian interpretation that Jesus’s brothers were Joseph’s children from an earlier marriage.
sources.title: The Brothers and Sisters of Jesus: An Epiphanian Response to John P. Meier
sources.citation: Richard Bauckham, “The Brothers and Sisters of Jesus: An Epiphanian Response to John P. Meier,” Catholic Biblical Quarterly 56 (1994), JSTOR 43721789.

position_map.label: Cousins or other close kin
position_map.summary: cousins/other close relatives, classically children of another Mary.
position_map.attestation: Jerome’s Against Helvidius argues that the named brothers were cousins or other relatives rather than Mary’s children; the USCCB note on Mark 6 records the broader-kin interpretation.

sources.claim_supported: Jerome argues that the people called Jesus’s brothers were cousins or other relatives and connects them with another Mary.
sources.title: The Perpetual Virginity of Blessed Mary: Against Helvidius
sources.citation: Jerome, Against Helvidius, English translation hosted in the New Advent Fathers collection.

sources.claim_supported: The USCCB note on Mark 6:3 records an interpretation of the brothers and sisters as close relations rather than necessarily children of Mary.
sources.title: Mark, chapter 6
sources.citation: New American Bible, Revised Edition, note to Mark 6:3, United States Conference of Catholic Bishops.
